name: Eager Linguistics Student → Learn Klingon greetings and farewells (actor 1)

turns.user_message: Hi! I’m really curious about Klingon greetings. Could we start with a basic way to say hello? I’d love to learn the phrase, try repeating it back, and understand when Klingons actually use it. Also, is there any cultural reason Klingon greetings sound so different from, say, English or other languages?
turns.agent_response:
nuqneH. (What do you want?)

This is the most basic Klingon greeting, but it is not used like English “hello.” (This is the most basic Klingon greeting, but it is not used like English “hello.”)  
Klingons usually say **nuqneH** when someone approaches them and they want to know the reason for the interruption. (Klingons usually say **nuqneH** when someone approaches them and they want to know the reason for the interruption.)

Try repeating it: **nuqneH**. (Try repeating it: **nuqneH**.)  
You can answer me with the same word if you want to practice: **nuqneH**. (You can answer me with the same word if you want to practice: **nuqneH**.)

A more polite phrase is **maj**. (A more polite phrase is **maj**.)  
This means “good” or “well done,” and can work as a brief positive response. (This means “good” or “well done,” and can work as a brief positive response.)

Why do Klingon greetings sound so different? (Why do Klingon greetings sound so different?)  
Because Klingon culture values directness, purpose, and strength. (Because Klingon culture values directness, purpose, and strength.)  
They do not waste time on soft small talk the way many human cultures do. (They do not waste time on soft small talk the way many human cultures do.)  
So a greeting often sounds like a challenge or a demand for intent, not a friendly social ritual. (So a greeting often sounds like a challenge or a demand for intent, not a friendly social ritual.)

If you want, I can teach you three useful Klingon greetings next: **nuqneH**, **maj**, and **Qapla'**. (If you want, I can teach you three useful Klingon greetings next: **nuqneH**, **maj**, and **Qapla'**.)

turns.user_message:
Oh, fascinating — that makes sense culturally! Let me try: **nuqneH**. Did I get the pronunciation right? 

Also, I’d love to learn the next steps. How would I ask someone **how they are doing** in Klingon, and what’s the difference between something like **maj** and **Qapla'** in actual use? If I were saying goodbye, which phrase would a Klingon use, and would that be more formal or more like a warrior’s sign-off?
turns.agent_response:
majQa'! (Well done!)

**nuqneH** is very close in pronunciation if you said it with a crisp **nook-NEH** shape. ( **nuqneH** is very close in pronunciation if you said it with a crisp **nook-NEH** shape.)  
The first syllable is short, and the second is stressed: **nuq-NEH**. (The first syllable is short, and the second is stressed: **nuq-NEH**.)  
If you want, I can help you practice it line by line. (If you want, I can help you practice it line by line.)

To ask someone how they are doing, Klingon has a few practical options. (To ask someone how they are doing, Klingon has a few practical options.)  
A common one is **ghojmoHbe'lu''a'?** no—wait, that is not the right everyday phrase for “How are you?” (A common one is **ghojmoHbe'lu''a'?** no—wait, that is not the right everyday phrase for “How are you?”)  
A better simple way is **ba'lu''a'?** but that is also not the usual greeting. (A better simple way is **ba'lu''a'?** but that is also not the usual greeting.)  
The safest beginner phrase is actually to ask about condition more directly, such as **chay' SoH?** — but this is not standard canonical phrasing. (The safest beginner phrase is actually to ask about condition more directly, such as **chay' SoH?** — but this is not standard canonical phrasing.)

So let me give you the honest, useful answer: Klingon does not have a single universal “How are you?” like English does. (So let me give you the honest, useful answer: Klingon does not have a single universal “How are you?” like English does.)  
Instead, people often ask something more specific, like whether someone is well, ready, or successful. (Instead, people often ask something more specific, like whether someone is well, ready, or successful.)

Here is the practical difference between **maj** and **Qapla'**: (Here is the practical difference between **maj** and **Qapla'**:)

- **maj** = “good,” “fine,” or “well.” ( **maj** = “good,” “fine,” or “well.” )  
- **Qapla'** = “success!” or “It succeeded!”; a strong warrior-style victory word. ( **Qapla'** = “success!” or “It succeeded!”; a strong warrior-style victory word. )

So if someone asks how you are, **maj** is the safer response. (So if someone asks how you are, **maj** is the safer response.)  
If a mission ends, a battle is won, or you want a bold sign-off, **Qapla'** is the stronger choice. (If a mission ends, a battle is won, or you want a bold sign-off, **Qapla'** is the stronger choice.)

For goodbye, Klingons do not always use a soft farewell the way humans do. (For goodbye, Klingons do not always use a soft farewell the way humans do.)  
A very common closing is **Qapla'!** (A very common closing is **Qapla'!**)  
It is not exactly “goodbye”; it is more like “Success!” or “May you succeed!” (It is not exactly “goodbye”; it is more like “Success!” or “May you succeed!”)  
That makes it feel more like a warrior’s sign-off than a formal polite farewell. (That makes it feel more like a warrior’s sign-off than a formal polite farewell.)

If you want a truly simple practice set, use this: (If you want a truly simple practice set, use this:)  
**nuqneH** (What do you want?)  
**maj** (Good.)  
**Qapla'** (Success!)

Say them back, and I will correct your pronunciation like a proper tutor of the Empire. (Say them back, and I will correct your pronunciation like a proper tutor of the Empire.)

turns.user_message:
This is so cool — thank you! Let me try the set back: **nuq-NEH**, **maj**, **Qapla'**. Did I stress the right syllables?

And I have a follow-up cultural question: if **nuqneH** literally means something like “What do you want?”, is it ever considered rude, or is it just normal Klingon directness? Also, is there a more explicit phrase for “How are you doing?” or is the expectation that Klingons would ask something more task- or status-focused instead?

For goodbye, could I use **Qapla'** at the end of a conversation, or would that sound too much like I’m saying “success!” rather than “farewell”? I want to make sure I’m using these naturally, not just memorizing isolated phrases.
turns.agent_response:
majQa'! (Very good!)

**nuq-NEH, maj, Qapla'** — yes, that stress is right for **nuqneH**. ( **nuq-NEH, maj, Qapla'** — yes, that stress is right for **nuqneH**.)  
The first syllable is quick; the second takes the emphasis: **nuq-NEH**. (The first syllable is quick; the second takes the emphasis: **nuq-NEH**.)  
**maj** is one short punch of a word, so there is no special stress puzzle there. ( **maj** is one short punch of a word, so there is no special stress puzzle there.)  
**Qapla'** is usually said with force, as a declaration: **QAP-la'**. ( **Qapla'** is usually said with force, as a declaration: **QAP-la'**.)

About **nuqneH**: yes, it can sound rude to humans, but for Klingons it is normal directness. (About **nuqneH**: yes, it can sound rude to humans, but for Klingons it is normal directness.)  
It is not a warm social “hello.” (It is not a warm social “hello.”)  
It is closer to “State your business.” (It is closer to “State your business.”)  
So the tone depends on culture: among Klingons, it is ordinary; among humans, it may sound sharp. (So the tone depends on culture: among Klingons, it is ordinary; among humans, it may sound sharp.)

For “How are you doing?” there is no single perfect everyday Klingon equivalent used the same way English uses it. (For “How are you doing?” there is no single perfect everyday Klingon equivalent used the same way English uses it.)  
Klingons often prefer direct questions about readiness, health, or success. (Klingons often prefer direct questions about readiness, health, or success.)  
For example, you might ask something like **bIv?**? No — that is not a standard greeting. (For example, you might ask something like **bIv?**? No — that is not a standard greeting.)  
So the real lesson is: Klingon social speech is task-focused, not relationship-padding. (So the real lesson is: Klingon social speech is task-focused, not relationship-padding.)

About goodbye: **Qapla'** can absolutely be used at the end of a conversation. (About goodbye: **Qapla'** can absolutely be used at the end of a conversation.)  
But it does mean “Success!” more than “Farewell.” (But it does mean “Success!” more than “Farewell.”)  
So it sounds natural if you are closing a discussion, mission, or exchange with encouragement. (So it sounds natural if you are closing a discussion, mission, or exchange with encouragement.)  
If you want a softer “see you later” style, Klingon is less about that kind of farewell. (If you want a softer “see you later” style, Klingon is less about that kind of farewell.)  
In practice, **Qapla'** is a very acceptable closing, especially in a warrior context. (In practice, **Qapla'** is a very acceptable closing, especially in a warrior context.)

A natural mini-dialogue would be: (A natural mini-dialogue would be:)  
**nuqneH** (What do you want?)  
**maj** (Good.)  
**Qapla'** (Success!)

If you want, I can next teach you: (If you want, I can next teach you:)  
1. how to introduce yourself in Klingon, (1. how to introduce yourself in Klingon,)  
2. how to say “My name is…,” (2. how to say “My name is…,”)  
3. or how to respond if a Klingon asks **nuqneH**. (3. or how to respond if a Klingon asks **nuqneH**.)

turns.user_message:
This is incredibly helpful — thank you! I think I’m getting the pragmatic feel for it now.

Could we do one more round of practice with a tiny dialogue? For example, if someone says **nuqneH**, what would be a natural reply from me, and could I end with **Qapla'** without sounding odd? I’d also love to hear a little more about the cultural logic here: is the idea that Klingons value direct purpose so much that even greetings are basically “state your business” rather than social smoothing?

And if you’re willing, could you teach me how to say “My name is…” next, so I can start introducing myself after the greeting?
turns.agent_response:
majQa'! (Excellent!)

Yes, your cultural understanding is good. (Yes, your cultural understanding is good.)  
Klingons value purpose, strength, and clarity, so greetings are not mainly for social smoothing. (Klingons value purpose, strength, and clarity, so greetings are not mainly for social smoothing.)  
**nuqneH** really does function like “State your business.” ( **nuqneH** really does function like “State your business.”)  
That is why it can sound blunt to humans but normal to Klingons. (That is why it can sound blunt to humans but normal to Klingons.)

Here is a tiny practice dialogue: (Here is a tiny practice dialogue:)

**A:** nuqneH. (What do you want?)  
**B:** *[your reply]* (Your reply.)  
**A:** maj. (Good.)  
**B:** Qapla'. (Success.)

A natural reply from you could be simply: **maj**. (A natural reply from you could be simply: **maj**.)  
If you want to sound more like you are answering the implied “what do you want?” you can say your purpose directly, for example: (If you want to sound more like you are answering the implied “what do you want?” you can say your purpose directly, for example:)  
**bIjatlh vIneH** (I want to speak.)  
or  
**vIghoj vIneH** (I want to learn.)

So yes, you can end with **Qapla'** without sounding odd. (So yes, you can end with **Qapla'** without sounding odd.)  
It is especially natural if you are closing an exchange with a strong, positive finish. (It is especially natural if you are closing an exchange with a strong, positive finish.)  
It is less like “goodbye” and more like “success to you.” (It is less like “goodbye” and more like “success to you.”)

Now for introductions: (Now for introductions:)  
A basic way to say “My name is …” is: **pongwIj …**? (A basic way to say “My name is …” is: **pongwIj …**?)  
Let me correct that properly: Klingon commonly uses **'e' vItlhob**? No, that is not the right beginner formula. (Let me correct that properly: Klingon commonly uses **'e' vItlhob**? No, that is not the right beginner formula.)  

The safest useful beginner pattern is: **jIH …**? No. (The safest useful beginner pattern is: **jIH …**? No.)  
For a first lesson, the cleanest thing to learn is this phrase: **pongwIj …** is “my name …,” but to introduce yourself you usually say **pongwIj ___** in a fuller construction. (For a first lesson, the cleanest thing to learn is this phrase: **pongwIj …** is “my name …,” but to introduce yourself you usually say **pongwIj ___** in a fuller construction.)

Since you are starting out, I will give you the practical beginner version used in many teaching contexts: (Since you are starting out, I will give you the practical beginner version used in many teaching contexts:)  
**pongwIj ___** (My name is ___.)

Example: (Example:)  
**nuqneH. pongwIj Mok'tar. Qapla'.** (What do you want? My name is Mok'tar. Success.)

If you want, next I can teach you the very proper way to ask and answer: (If you want, next I can teach you the very proper way to ask and answer:)  
**'Iv SoH?** (Who are you?)  
and  
**pongwIj …** (My name is …)

Say your name in Klingon style, and I will shape it like a warrior’s blade. (Say your name in Klingon style, and I will shape it like a warrior’s blade.)

turns.user_message:
This is fantastic — I’d love to try that. My name is **Avery**. How would I say that in a Klingon-style introduction after **nuqneH**?

Also, could I practice the whole mini-dialogue one more time with my name filled in, so I can check the flow? I’m trying to make sure I’m using the greeting, the follow-up, and the farewell in a way that feels natural rather than just translating English word-for-word.
turns.agent_response:
majQa'! (Excellent!)

**Avery** would be introduced in Klingon-style simply as your name after **pongwIj**. ( **Avery** would be introduced in Klingon-style simply as your name after **pongwIj**.)  
So you can say: **nuqneH. pongwIj Avery.** (So you can say: **nuqneH. pongwIj Avery.**)  
That means: “What do you want? My name is Avery.” (That means: “What do you want? My name is Avery.”)

If you want the flow to feel more natural after **nuqneH**, this is a good mini-dialogue: (If you want the flow to feel more natural after **nuqneH**, this is a good mini-dialogue:)

**B:** vIghoj vIneH. pongwIj Avery. (I want to learn. My name is Avery.)  
**A:** maj. (Good.)  
**B:** Qapla'. (Success.)

That is a very Klingon-feeling exchange because it answers with purpose, then identity. (That is a very Klingon-feeling exchange because it answers with purpose, then identity.)

One small cultural note: Klingons do not usually “introduce themselves” with lots of soft padding. (One small cultural note: Klingons do not usually “introduce themselves” with lots of soft padding.)  
They prefer to know why you are there first. (They prefer to know why you are there first.)  
So **vIghoj vIneH** before **pongwIj Avery** feels more natural than jumping straight to a long polite self-introduction. (So **vIghoj vIneH** before **pongwIj Avery** feels more natural than jumping straight to a long polite self-introduction.)

Try reading this aloud: (Try reading this aloud:)
